California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
January 6, 1992
Thomas A. Kathe
city Attorney
77 Fair Drive
Costa Mesa, cA 92624-1200
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
our File No. I -91- 507
Dear l'1r. Kathe l
This letter is in response to your request for advice
regarding the duties and respons ibi I it ies of City of costa Mesa
councilmember Peter Buffa, chairman of the costa Mesa
Redevelopment Agency, under the conf I ict-of- interest provisions of
the Political Reforn Act (the rrActrr).r Your request is general in
nature and does not refer to a particular decision pending before
the city council or the redevelopment agency. Accordingly, we
treat your letter as a request for informal assistance pursuant to
the provisions of Regulation 18329.'
OUESTIONS
1. Do the provisions of Government code Section 1090
prohibit Councilmember Buffa, chairman of the costa Mesa
iedevelopment Agency, from participating in decisions regarding
Triangte square Joint venture, a developer of redevelopnent
propeity under a development agreement with the costa Mesa
ieaLvelopment Agency, where the developer enploys the principal
officers, as individuals, of a public relations corporation in
which Sharon Buffa is an enPloYee?
2. Do the conf I ict-of- interest provisions of the Act
disqualify Mr. Buffa, as a councilnenber or redeveLopnent agency
1 cor.r...nt 
Code Sections B1OOO-91015. Al.I statutory references
are to the Government code unless otherltise indicated. 
commission
regutations appear at 2 california Code of Regulations. Section
18ooo, et seq.- Al.I references to regulations are to Title 2,
oivision o of the california code of Requlations'
2 rnformal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice'
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329 (c) . )
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O7 o sacramento cA 958O4'O8O7 . (916) 322'5660


Our File No. I-9■
-507
Page 2
chairnan, fron participating in all Datters directly involving or
affecting Triangle Square Joint venture due to sharon Buffa's
employnent with a public relations corporation where the principal
officers, as individuals, are to be enployed by the devel-oper?
CONCLUS TONS
1. The Commission's advice is lirnited to the provisions of
the Act. 
We suggest that you consult with the Office of the
Attorney General. regarding applicability of the provisions of
Governnent code Section 1090 to your facts.
2. Mr. Buffa must disqualify himself from participating in
any governmental decision which r.ri11 foreseeably and materially
affect his spouse's employer, the Greensburgh croup, Inc., the
principals in the business, Ms. Roxburgh and Mr. creener, or any
of their related business entities.
FACTS
Peter Buffa is a City of costa Mesa counciLmenber and
Chairman of the costa Mesa Redevelopment Agency (the rragencyrr) .
The agency has approved a deveJ.opment agreement with Triangle
Square Joint Venture (TSJV), a developer, to participate in the
developnent of a parcel of property ovrned by the redeveLopment
agency as part of a redevelopnent plan for the downtolrn area of
the city. 
The developer's project consists of a shopping mall.
The mall is near completion.
sharon Buffa is the wife of councilr0enber Buffa. Since the
summer of 1991, !Irs. Buffa has been elnployed as a consultant by a
corporation known as the Greensburgh GrouP, Inc.. 
The corporation
was formed in early 1991 by claudia Roxburgh and GIen Greener, who
are its officers and shareholders. Ms. Roxburgh is the principal
owner of a public relations and advertising agency known as the
Roxburgh Agency. Mr. Greener is the principal ovJner of a
political consulting and public relations agency known as Greener
& Adams.
The Roxburgh Agency and Greener & Adans are separate business
entities with separate bank accounts. The two business entities
share a floor of offices but each entity pays its own share of the
leasing expenses. The Greensburgh Group, Inc. is a corporation
and a separate business fron the Roxburgh Agency and Greener &
Adans. ihe Greensburgh Group, Inc. has purchased services such as
art direction, layout, and typesetting fron the Roxburgh Agency at
favorable rates.
In october, 1991, the Roxburgh Agency expressed interest in
obtaining an agreetnent with the developer to provide public
relations and advertising services for the shopping ma11. The
proposed agreement would retain the Roxburgh Agency, and
i{r.-creenei as an individual. 
Neither the Greensburgh Group, rnc.
nor Mrs. Buffa would be employed by or perforn services for the


Our File No. ■-9■-507
Page 3
shopping ma1I, for the Roxburgh Agency, Mr. creener, or the
developer. Mr. Greener, the Roxburgh Agency, and the officers of
the developer have agreed to not enter into any agreement for
public retations and advertising services pending receipt of
written advice from the Commission regarding applicability of the
conf I ict-of- interest provisions of the Act to Ur. Buffa.
ANALYSI S
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits public officials 
from
making, participating in making, or in any $ray attenpting to use
their official 
position to influence a governnentaL decision in
which they know or have reason to know they have a financial
interest. 
As a councilmember and chairnan of the costa Mesa
Redevelopment Agency, Mr. Buffa is a public official 
subject to
the provisions of the Act. 
(Section 82048.) Thus, he may not use
his official 
position to influence a governnental decision in
which he knows,J or has reason to knov, he has a financial
interest.
A public officiaL makes a governmental decision $/hen the
official 
votes, makes an appointnent, connits an agency to a
course of action, enters into a contractual agreenent on behalf of
the agency, deterrnines not to act, negotiates, advises, or nakes
recornmendat ions to the decisionhaker. AdditionaIIy, where the
public official 
contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attenpts
to influence any nenber, officer, enployee or consultant of the
official's 
agency concerning a governnental decision, the official
is considered to have used his or her official 
position to
influence the decision. 
(Regulations 18700 and 18700.1.)
Accordingly, if Mr. Buffa is disgualified from participating in a
governrnental decision, he rnust not only abstain frorn voting on the
matter but he nust also abstain fron atternpting to influence the
decision by contacting members of the city council or the
redevelopment agency as vrelI as staff regarding the decision.
Financial Interests
For purposes of Section 87100,. a public official 
has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision wilI have a naterial financial effect,
3 An official knows that he or she has a financial interest in a
decision if the official actually knows that it is reasonab)'y
foreseeable that a decision r'rilL naterially affect a source of
income. As a general rule, an official rrhas reason to knowrr that
a decision wili affect a source of income whenever a reasonable
person, under the sane circunstances, vould be likely to know the
iaentiiy of the source of incorne and would be aware of the
aecisionrs probabLe inpact on the source. (Price Advice Letter,
No. A-88-479. )


Our Flle No. I-9■
-507
Page 4
distinguishable fron the effect on the public generally, -on the
official, 
or on a member of his or her inatediate family,' or on:
Any source of ineome, other than gifts and
other than loans by a connercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the publ,ic without regard to
official 
status, aggregating tlro hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or nore in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 nonths prior to the tirne when the
decision is nade.
Section 8 7103 (c) .
Councilnenber Buffa's incorne includes any community property
interest in the incorne of his spouse. (Section 82030.)
Consequently, Mr. Buffa nust disqualify hirnself fron participating
in any decision regarding a source of incone of 9500 or more to
Mrs. Buffa within 12 nonths of any decision regarding the source
of incorne if it is reasonably foreseeable that the source of
incone will be affected uraterially by a governmental decision.
(Shaw Advice Letter, No. I-91-108.) In addition, Regulation 18706
provides that an official 
has a financiaf interest in a decision
vrithin the meaning of section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision wiII have a material financial
effect, distinguishable fron its effect on the public generally,
on a business entity which is otherwise reLated to a business
entitv in which the official 
has a financial interest. 
lvaqin
Advice Letter, No. I-89-688. )
Regulation 18236 defines an I'otherwise related business
entity" in relevant part as follows:
(b) Business entities, including corpora-
tions, partnerships, joint ventures and any other
organizations and enterprises operated for profit,
which do not have a parent-subsidiary relationship
are otherwise related if any one of the following
three tests is rnet:
(1) One business entitY has a
controlling ownership interest in the other
business entity.
(2) There is shared rnanagement and
control betuteen the entities. 
In determining
$/hether there is shared management and
control, consideration shouLd be given to the
following factors:
4 section 82029 defines "iumediate fanilytt to include an
official's 
spouse and dePendent children.


Our File NO. I-9■-507
Page 5
(A) The same person or
substantially the same person owns and
manages the two entities;
(B) There are conmon or cornrningled
funds or assets;
(C) The business entities share the
use of the same offices or employees, or
otherwise share activities, 
resources or
personnel on a regular basis;
(D) There is otherwise a regular
and close working relationship between
the entities; or
(3) A controlling o$/ner (50t or greater
interest as a shareholder or as a general
partner) 5 in one entity atso is a controlling
owner in the other entity.
Regulation 18236 (b) and (c),
footnote added.
we now determine whether the various business interests of
l4r. Roxburgh and Mr. Greener are rrother$/ise related business
entities" under the provisions of the Act. !lr. Buffa's spouse is
an enployee of the creensburgh Group, Inc., a corporation. 
For
purposes of our analysis we assune that Ms. Buffa has received in
excess of S5oo in salary fron her employer. Claudia Roxburgh and
clen Greener are the officers and shareholders of the Greensburgh
Group, Inc.. 
For purposes of our analysis we also assurne that
Ms. Roxburgh and l,tr. Greener olrn equal shares in the Greensburgh
croup, Inc,. 
Ms. Roxburgh is the principal owner of the Roxburgh
Agency, while Mr. creener is the principal owner of Greener &
Adams. You seek our advice to determine r.rhether Mr. Buf f a woul.d
be disqualified from participating in decisions regarding TSJV and
the shopping mall if Roxburgh Agency and Mr. Greener entered into
a contract with TsJv to provide public relations and advertising
services for the shopping mall.
Applying the test of Regulation 18236, it wouLd appear that
the various business interests of Ms. Roxburgh and Mr. Greener are
Iotherwise related business entities.rr This is so because
Ms. Roxburgh and I'1r. Greener, who are the controlling owners of
the creensburgh Group, Inc., are also the controlling owners of
the Roxburgh Agency and Greener & Adams respectively. 
(Regulation
18235(b)(3).) Moreover, the Roxburgh Agency and Greener & Adams
5  .n ■985′
controlling
than 501.
The Cornrnission adopted a
ownership interest as an
(vaoin, supra. )
policy interpreting a
ownership interest of nore


Our File No.

share a floor of offices and the creensburgh croup, Inc. has
purchased services from the Roxburgh Agency at favorable rates.
(Regulation 18236(b)(2).) Accordingly, if Claudia Roxburgh or
clen Greener were to contract with TSJV through any of their
businesses or in their individual capacity, Mr. Buffa,s
disqua 1j. f ication $rou1d be required if any governmental, decision
would have a reasonably foreseeable rnaterial financial effect on
these individuals or on any of their business interests discussed
above .
Poreseeabilitv
The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. To be
foreseeable, the effect of a decision nust be nore than a nere
possibility; 
ho$rever, certainty is not required. 
(Doh,nev cares v.
Dolrnev Conmunitv Developnent Comn. (1987) 196 CaI.App.3d 983, 989-
991; Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal.App.3d 817; In re Thorner (1975)
1 FPPC Ops, 198.) The Act seeks to prevent more than actual
conflicts of interesti it seeks to prevent even the appearance of
a possible conflict of interest. 
(witt v. Morrovr, supra at 823.)
It is foreseeable that entering into a contract to provide
services to TSJV will have a financial effect on Ms. Roxburgh and
Mr. Greener who are sources of incorne to Mr. Buffa. This is so
because they vriIl receive income for their services. If the
effect is naterial, Mr. Buffa's disqualification will be required
unless the trpublic generally" exception discussed below appiies.
Mater ia 1 itv
Regulation 18702 sets forth the guidelines for deterrnining
whether an official's 
financial interest in a deci.sion is
ItmateriaL'r as required by Section 87103. If the officialrs
financial interest is directlv involved in the decision, then
Regulation lA702. t applies to deterrnine materiality. 
If, on the
other hand, the official's 
interest would be indirectlv affected
by the decision, then Regulations 18702,2 through f8702,6 would
apply to determine whether the effect of the decision is material,
as discussed below.
An official's 
economic interest is directJ,y before the
official,s 
agency v/hen the source of incorne, either personally or
by an agent:
(1) fnitiates the proceeding in which the
decision will be made by filing an application,
cIain, appeat, or similar request or;
(2) Is a naned party in, or is the subject
of, the proceeding concerning the decision before
the official 
or the official's 
agency.


Our File No. I-9■
-507
Page 7
(3) A person or business entity is the
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation
of any license, perrnit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the subject Person or bus j.ness
entity.
Regulation L8702.1(b) .
Ms. Roxburgh and Mr. Greener are l'trs. Buffa's employers
through creensburgh Group, Inc., a corporation of which they are
the officers and shareholders. The Roxburgh Agency is a business
entity owned by Ms. Roxburgh. Therefore, as stated above, these
business interests are rrotherlrise related business entitiesrt for
purposes of the Act. when it is reasonably foreseeable that a
governmental decision wj.Il have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public AeneraIly, on a
business entity which is a parent or subsidiary of, or is
otherwise related to, a business entity which is a source of
incorne to the official, 
the official 
has a financial interest in
the decision within the rneaning of Section 87100. (Regulation
18706.) Accordingly, Mr. Buffa nust disqualify hinself from
participating in decisions which viII foreseeably and naterially
affect any of these related business entities.
These rtotherwise related business entitLes" would not be
directly before Mr. Buffa's agency if the Roxburgh agency and
Mr. Greener contracted with TSJV to provide services for the
shopping rna1J.. However, decisions of the city council or of the
redLveloprnent agency regarding TSJV may affect these econonic
interests of Mr. Buffa indirectly.
when a business entity in which a public official' has a
financial interest is affected indirectly by a governmental
decision, the appropriate standard for deternining materiality is
that of Regulation L87o2.2. For all but the largest business
entities, [he appropriate standard is that of Subdivision (9) of
Regulation La702.2 which states that the effect of a decision is
rnaierial as to a business entity in. which a Public official 
has an
economic interest if :
(1) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $10,000 or tnore; or
(2) The decision wilt result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or
(3) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of S10,000 or more.


Our Flle No. I-9■-507
Page 8
Accordingly, j,f you deternine that any decisions regarding TsJv
will indirectly affect the Greensburgh Group, Ltd., the Roxburgh
Agency or Greener & Adans j,n the anounts stated above, Mr. Buffa's
disqualification r,rould be required unless the "public generally
exception applies.
Public GeneraI lv
Even if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
decision is material, disqua I i f ication is required only if the
effect is distinguishable fron the effect on the public genera),Iy.
(Section 87100.) If the decision does not affect all members of
the public in the same manner, disqual if ication may be required
unless the effect of the decision on the source of income is the
same as the effect on a significant segment of the public.
(Regulation 18703. )
The rpublic generally" exception is inapplicable to your
facts. this is so because any contractual arrangetnent between
TsJv and Ms. Roxburgh or Hr. Greener will affect their interests
but $/i11 not affect other nenbers of the community who reside in
the City of costa l,lesa.

18235(b)(3).) Moreover, the Roxburgh Agency and Greener & Adams

share a floor of offices and the creensburgh croup, Inc. has

(Regulation 18236(b)(2).) Accordingly, if Claudia Roxburgh or
clen Greener were to contract with TSJV through any of their

disqua 1j. f ication $rou1d be required if any governmental, decision
would have a reasonably foreseeable rnaterial financial effect on
these individuals or on any of their business interests discussed
above .
Poreseeabilitv
The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. To be
foreseeable, the effect of a decision nust be nore than a nere
possibility; 
ho$rever, certainty is not required. 
(Doh,nev cares v.
Dolrnev Conmunitv Developnent Comn. (1987) 196 CaI.App.3d 983, 989-
991; Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal.App.3d 817; In re Thorner (1975)
1 FPPC Ops, 198.) The Act seeks to prevent more than actual
conflicts of interesti it seeks to prevent even the appearance of
a possible conflict of interest. 
(witt v. Morrovr, supra at 823.)
It is foreseeable that entering into a contract to provide
services to TSJV will have a financial effect on Ms. Roxburgh and
Mr. Greener who are sources of incorne to Mr. Buffa. This is so
because they vriIl receive income for their services. If the
effect is naterial, Mr. Buffa's disqualification will be required
unless the trpublic generally" exception discussed below appiies.
Mater ia 1 itv
Regulation 18702 sets forth the guidelines for deterrnining
whether an official's 
financial interest in a deci.sion is
ItmateriaL'r as required by Section 87103. If the officialrs
financial interest is directlv involved in the decision, then
Regulation lA702. t applies to deterrnine materiality. 
If, on the
other hand, the official's 
interest would be indirectlv affected
by the decision, then Regulations 18702,2 through f8702,6 would
apply to determine whether the effect of the decision is material,
as discussed below.
An official's 
economic interest is directJ,y before the
official,s 
agency v/hen the source of incorne, either personally or
by an agent:
(1) fnitiates the proceeding in which the
decision will be made by filing an application,
cIain, appeat, or similar request or;

of, the proceeding concerning the decision before
the official 
or the official's 
agency.

-507
(3) A person or business entity is the
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation
of any license, perrnit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the subject Person or bus j.ness
entity.
Regulation L8702.1(b) .
Ms. Roxburgh and Mr. Greener are l'trs. Buffa's employers
through creensburgh Group, Inc., a corporation of which they are
the officers and shareholders. The Roxburgh Agency is a business
entity owned by Ms. Roxburgh. Therefore, as stated above, these
business interests are rrotherlrise related business entitiesrt for
purposes of the Act. when it is reasonably foreseeable that a

distinguishable from its effect on the public AeneraIly, on a
business entity which is a parent or subsidiary of, or is
otherwise related to, a business entity which is a source of
incorne to the official, 
the official 
has a financial interest in
the decision within the rneaning of Section 87100. (Regulation
18706.) Accordingly, Mr. Buffa nust disqualify hinself from
participating in decisions which viII foreseeably and naterially
affect any of these related business entities.
These rtotherwise related business entitLes" would not be
directly before Mr. Buffa's agency if the Roxburgh agency and
Mr. Greener contracted with TSJV to provide services for the
shopping rna1J.. However, decisions of the city council or of the
redLveloprnent agency regarding TSJV may affect these econonic
interests of Mr. Buffa indirectly.
when a business entity in which a public official' has a
financial interest is affected indirectly by a governmental
decision, the appropriate standard for deternining materiality is
that of Regulation L87o2.2. For all but the largest business
entities, [he appropriate standard is that of Subdivision (9) of
Regulation La702.2 which states that the effect of a decision is
rnaierial as to a business entity in. which a Public official 
has an
economic interest if :
(1) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $10,000 or tnore; or
(2) The decision wilt result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or
(3) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of S10,000 or more.

Accordingly, j,f you deternine that any decisions regarding TsJv
will indirectly affect the Greensburgh Group, Ltd., the Roxburgh
Agency or Greener & Adans j,n the anounts stated above, Mr. Buffa's
disqualification r,rould be required unless the "public generally
exception applies.
Public GeneraI lv
Even if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
decision is material, disqua I i f ication is required only if the
effect is distinguishable fron the effect on the public genera),Iy.
(Section 87100.) If the decision does not affect all members of
the public in the same manner, disqual if ication may be required
unless the effect of the decision on the source of income is the
same as the effect on a significant segment of the public.
(Regulation 18703. )
The rpublic generally" exception is inapplicable to your
facts. this is so because any contractual arrangetnent between
TsJv and Ms. Roxburgh or Hr. Greener will affect their interests
but $/i11 not affect other nenbers of the community who reside in
the City of costa l,lesa.

Kathe l This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the duties and respons ibi I it ies of City of costa Mesa councilmember Peter Buffa, chairman of the costa Mesa Redevelopment Agency, under the conf I ict-of- interest provisions of the Political Reforn Act (the rrActrr).r Your request is general in nature and does not refer to a particular decision pending before the city council or the redevelopment agency. Accordingly, we treat your letter as a request for informal assistance pursuant to the provisions of Regulation 18329.' OUESTIONS 1. Do the provisions of Government code Section 1090 prohibit Councilmember Buffa, chairman of the costa Mesa iedevelopment Agency, from participating in decisions regarding Triangte square Joint venture, a developer of redevelopnent propeity under a development agreement with the costa Mesa ieaLvelopment Agency, where the developer enploys the principal officers, as individuals, of a public relations corporation in which Sharon Buffa is an enPloYee? 2. Mr. Buffa must disqualify himself from participating in any governmental decision which r.ri11 foreseeably and materially affect his spouse's employer, the Greensburgh croup, Inc., the principals in the business, Ms. Roxburgh and Mr. creener, or any of their related business entities. FACTS Peter Buffa is a City of costa Mesa counciLmenber and Chairman of the costa Mesa Redevelopment Agency (the rragencyrr) . The agency has approved a deveJ.opment agreement with Triangle Square Joint Venture (TSJV), a developer, to participate in the developnent of a parcel of property ovrned by the redeveLopment agency as part of a redevelopnent plan for the downtolrn area of the city.
